title: Paraparticles intrinsically exhibit Hardy-space breakdown
abstract: The memory kernel of an open quantum system obeys Kramers--Kronig (KK) relations if and only if its Laplace transform is analytic in the upper half-plane -- a property known as Hardy-space analyticity. Here we show that non-unitary exchange statistics, the defining property of paraparticles, intrinsically breaks Hardy-space analyticity. The metric $η$ that guarantees a real closed-system spectrum for these particles necessarily differs from the physical Born inner product ($\|η- I\|_F / \|I\|_F = 0.51$) -- a mathematical consequence of the R-matrix's non-unitarity, not a parameter choice. This metric is a "shadow metric": Schur's lemma forces it to commute with every bilinear observable, making the distortion physically invisible in the closed system. But when the paraparticle is coupled to a bath, any coupling operator that lies outside the symmetry algebra -- that is, any interaction that sees the internal flavour structure -- exposes the distortion. The memory kernel then develops upper-half-plane poles at coupling $g_c \approx 0.1$, breaking standard dispersion relations before the closed-system spectrum complexifies. Fermions and bosons, whose exchange is unitary ($η= I$ as an analytic fact of the canonical anticommutation algebra), are immune at any coupling, because there is no distortion to expose. The violation is intrinsic: it distinguishes non-unitary exchange statistics from ordinary particle statistics at the level of the memory kernel's analytic structure.
comment: 8 pages main + 13 pages Supplemental Material, 3 figures. Data and code: [Liu, K. (2026). Data and code for "Paraparticles intrinsically exhibit Hardy-space breakdown" [Dataset]. Zenodo. https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21342378

bite.hook: Paraparticles intrinsically break Hardy-space analyticity, exposing a hidden 0.51 metric distortion.
tldr: The author investigated the mathematical properties of the memory kernel in open quantum systems containing paraparticles.
They proved that non-unitary exchange statistics in paraparticles force a metric distortion that breaks Hardy-space analyticity.
They found that while this distortion is invisible in closed systems, coupling to an external bath exposes it, breaking standard dispersion relations.
bite.whyItMatters: In physics, we assume all fundamental particles are either bosons or fermions. Paraparticles are theoretical particles with exotic 'middle-ground' statistics; this paper reveals a fundamental mathematical breakdown in how they interact with their environments, offering a new way to theoretically test or rule out their existence.
bite.eli5: Imagine a toy that looks perfectly normal when sitting on a shelf, but as soon as you plug it into a wall outlet, it behaves in a bizarre, forbidden way. Paraparticles are like this toy—their weird math is hidden until they interact with the outside world.

keyNumbers.label: Metric distortion value
keyNumbers.context: The calculated difference between the metric eta and the physical Born inner product

keyNumbers.label: Critical coupling constant
keyNumbers.context: The coupling strength gc where the memory kernel develops upper-half-plane poles

glossary.shortDef: Hypothetical particles that obey parastatistics, generalizing the familiar Bose-Einstein and Fermi-Dirac statistics.
glossary.eli5Def: Exotic, theoretical particles that do not follow the normal rules of being either social (like bosons) or antisocial (like fermions).

glossary.shortDef: A property where a function's Laplace transform is analytic (smooth and well-behaved) in the upper half-plane.
glossary.eli5Def: A mathematical rule ensuring that cause always precedes effect in physical systems, meaning things cannot happen before they are triggered.

glossary.shortDef: Mathematical properties connecting the real and imaginary parts of certain complex functions, rooted in causality.
glossary.eli5Def: A set of math rules that link how a material absorbs energy to how it bends light, ensuring the universe respects cause-and-effect.
